questions.question: What is 'habit stacking,' as described in the lesson?

options.text: Doing all three resilience habits at the same time to save time
Linking a new habit to an existing habit you already do automatically
Gradually increasing how long you practice a habit each day
Completing all your wellness habits in one dedicated block each morning

questions.explanation: Habit stacking uses an existing routine as an anchor for a new behavior. The formula is: 'After/Before [current habit], I will [new habit].' For example, 'After I pour my morning coffee, I will sit and do 10 mindful breaths.' This makes new habits much easier to start because they piggyback on something you already do automatically.

questions.question: Why does the lesson recommend a gratitude practice as a resilience habit?

options.text: Gratitude eliminates negative emotions by replacing them with positive ones
Anxiety makes our brains scan for threats, and gratitude intentionally shifts focus to what is good, rewiring the brain over time
It is a social activity that reduces isolation
It increases productivity and reduces procrastination

questions.explanation: Anxiety hijacks your brain's threat-scanning system, keeping it focused on what could go wrong. Gratitude practice is a deliberate counterweight — it trains your brain to notice what is going well, rewiring it to spot positives more readily over time. This does not erase problems; it creates a more balanced perspective.

questions.question: What is a 'relapse prevention plan' and when should you create it?

options.text: A plan you make after a setback to figure out what went wrong
A plan created when you are calm, in advance, so you know exactly what to do when a hard day arrives
A plan your therapist creates for you to follow
A decision to avoid the situations that trigger your anxiety

questions.explanation: Think of a relapse prevention plan as a fire escape plan for your mind. You create it while you are calm — when you have access to your full thinking brain — so it is ready to use during a hard day when you cannot think clearly. Having it prepared transforms a potential crisis into a planned-for event, which itself reduces anxiety.

questions.question: What are 'early warning signs' in the context of anxiety management?

options.text: The most severe symptoms that signal you are in a full crisis
The subtle, personal signals in your body, thoughts, or behavior that anxiety is beginning to ramp up
External events or stressors that are likely to cause anxiety in the future
Symptoms that only appear during a panic attack

questions.explanation: Early warning signs are the first subtle, personal signals that your anxiety is starting to increase — things like trouble sleeping for a few nights, starting to avoid phone calls, or your shoulders feeling tight. Knowing YOUR specific warning signs allows you to reach for your tools early, before the storm fully hits.

questions.question: Using the habit stacking formula from this lesson, write your personal plan: 'After/Before [current habit], I will [new habit].' Which resilience habit — mindfulness, gratitude, or self-compassion — are you choosing, and what existing daily routine will you attach it to?
questions.explanation: The best habit stack is one that is small enough to feel almost too easy. A 1-minute practice attached to something you already do every day beats a 20-minute practice you attempt twice and abandon. Start absurdly small. The consistency of daily practice — not the duration — is what creates lasting change in your brain's resilience.
